speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 4, July 12, 1995, interview with Izchak Goldblatt. Let's go back to Auschwitz for a moment or two. There was a-- an episode there that you wanted to talk about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I-- I-- I'm very surprised. This is the one I should have told the first, and I'm surprised that I forgot all about it while I was talking about Auschwitz. This is what it does to you when you-- when you talk about it. When you relive it, you-- you get so excited that you don't know what exactly to talk about. I remember an incident where suddenly somebody yelled out, look, look! And we turned around, and we saw, near the ladies, where the bath was, some ladies came out, and one of them had a baby in her arms. And one of the SS men went to her and took the lady, grabbed the lady-- the baby out of her-- of the lady's hands and threw the baby up in the air. And like four-- four SS men shot their revolvers. They shot through the baby to see who can shoot her more times. And they laughed, and they enjoyed themselves. This was like, what shall I say, 200 feet away from us, maybe 250 feet. But it was a very clear picture, very clearly so. Unbelievable. And we were still fresh from home, and I-- I couldn't believe that something like this is happening. So somebody said, yeah, it must have been something else. It's not the baby. So immediate-- and you wanted to believe that, that it was not the baby. And-- and I recall an instant after liberation, when I was with-- with-- in an American field hospital, one of the male nurses, a guy by the name of Larry McCormick, from New Jersey somewhere, that's all I remember about him. He came back from the crematorium, and he said, Izchak, I have taken photographs, but I can't believe it's true. There was-- in Ebensee, they didn't-- they-- they could only burn 500 bodies a day, but there were more dead in a day than 500, so they stacked a huge room, five and five, five bodies over five bodies. Five in length, five in large. And there were some of them-- and he went into that room, and he took pictures. He said some of them were still moving a leg or a hand. Some of them were still saying, oy. And he said, and this is only like five, six minutes walk from the tent where-- where I was. And-- and he said, Izchak, I cannot believe it's true. Because the human mind cannot accept. In the same way, and I remember now, when I think of it, this is why we believed, no, it must have been a rag doll that they were throwing out. It must have not been a baby. It couldn't have been. Sure enough, it was a baby. I was young enough and had very good eyes-- at that time I was 17 years old-- to see 200 or 250 feet from me that it was a live baby that they throw out. I can't tell if it's a boy or a girl, but it was a live baby that they threw in the air, and four of them shot through that baby to see how many times they can shoot it. I think that's all I remember about Auschwitz. And I don't even want to remember more, because it-- it-- it cost me-- it cost me too much to remember. I would rather go back to where we were at the last--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you said that you want to talk about Wolfsberg, some sort of funny human incidents.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to go and tell that there were funny moments. And maybe I will also tell you something about spiritual moments that are unimaginable and unbelievable in Wolfsberg. One day, these brothers, these-- these-- these lager brother, this camp brother that I had, his name is Tully Lazerovitch. He still lives in Israel today. These organizer who-- who could make some food of nothing, from under the earth, literally, when we would walk to work, he would jump out of the line. I mean, they shot you immediately. They-- they-- they would beat you to death if they catch you. And he would go into the field, dig up something, and would come back with a blouse full of potatoes and then open my blouse and put-- stuff potatoes into them. And-- unbelievable how these men dared to do things.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, he comes and says, Itzo, ooh, I have good news. What is the good news? He said, remember, you have gold coins at home. You left gold coins at home. Now you have to tell there is a boy from our town who works in the kitchen. His name is Shier. You have to promise him that you're going to pay him. For every bowl of soup extra that he's going to give you, you're going to pay him a gold coin when we come home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he brings me a piece of paper torn from a-- from a cement sack, because there wasn't paper there, and a-- a small pencil, and I write my name, I, Izchak Goldblatt am going to give Shia-- so I don't remember his name-- Shia Berger, a coin for every bowl of soup that he's going to give me. And I signed it with my full signature, and he took it away to this Shia, and sure enough, for about two weeks, every day after the soup where we were in the camp, we got another soup from this guy, from this Shia. This was-- you can't imagine what a bowl of soup means in-- in-- in Wolfsberg, in-- in a labor camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: After two weeks, this Tully Lazerovitch comes and says, Itzo says bitter. What is bitter? He said, there is a guy from our city, from our town, from Sighet. His name is Otzi Mendelovich, and he promised Shia two coins he's going to give him every day. And Shia starts giving the soup to Otzi. So he says now, take another piece of paper, and you promise him three coins every day. So he gave me another piece of paper, and I write. And while I am writing, he says, write four coins, so that he cannot compete with you-- with-- with us. And sure enough, I wrote four coins for every bowl of soup. I would give him four coins. And after the war, I go to [NON-ENGLISH], to my rabbi, and I meet this Shia there, and we look at each other, and we hug each other, and we both start laughing, burst out into laughter to say that I promised him, where am I going to get the gold coins? I never had any gold coins. Where am I going to get them? Didn't he know that we didn't have gold coins? But he wanted to believe the gold coins, I wanted to-- to have the soup. So I find this is a very funny situation that some funny situations would-- would happen in-- in-- in the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: A small, another funny situation that happened in the camp, in Ebensee. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were working about-- this was like 10 days before-- before-- at the end of April in 1945. This-- we were working outside. We were working on a platform where we were bringing-- we were building railways. Now, a rail like this, we would catch like six people. Each one would pick it up and throw it down on the floor from the platform. Now, I was so weak, I was weighing like 35 kilos at the time, which is like 75 pounds, I would say, or something like that. I was so weak that I couldn't withhold. I was falling with that rail. Each rail that we picked up, I was falling with the rail. And the-- the-- the German who work-- was the overseer, would pick me up at the bottom of the pants like a bundle, and put me back on there. And everybody would laugh. It was so funny. And now, you think about it, how weak I was, and-- and this was a funny situation. We were laughing about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I would like--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about spiritual moments too.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to tell about a very particular spiritual-- heightened spiritual experience that we had in-- that I had in camp. And this was every Friday night after the meal, we would come into one of the tents, and we were singing zemirot songs, shabbat songs, with tears is running from our eyes. The-- the closeness to God that we felt, never before and never after have I felt. And I was always a very religious boy. Never before and never after have I felt so close to God. He was visible and tangible. Almost you could reach Him, you could touch Him. That close we felt to God. And this wasn't only me, but all of us in that tent. We were praying the-- the shabbat prayer with such ferver that the biggest of rabbis cannot pray so-- so. And I cannot imagine. A reminder of that kind of something similar, something close to that experience I had about a month ago or a month and a half ago at [NON-ENGLISH], on an-- Israel Independence Day. That night, I spoke in South Shore in the yeshiva for-- before the high school kids. I spoke in front of 120 kids, from 15 to 18, with their rabbis. And there was one lady, I don't know how she belonged there. She was sitting at the end of the lines. And I spoke to them. I was supposed to speak for like 20 minutes, but when I saw the feedback that I got from them, and I even told them that I feel [SPEAKS HEBREW], a-- a-- a heightening of-- of-- of a-- of a-- of a religious experience. I feel here almost like I felt in the camps Friday nights when we were there. It was-- it was-- I was very grateful that I had the occasion to speak to these boys. I spoke to them about my experience in Auschwitz. Very little I spoke about the camps. I gave them a little bit of the great Torah, Talmudic stories which I-- which I like to tell. They loved it. I saw them, and I forgot the time. I spoke for an hour and five minutes. And then abruptly, I stopped, saying to them, I could go on for four hours more, but I think I've made the impression on you, and I will leave it with that. And that's how I got-- and they were standing up and giving-- giving me a standing ovation like five or six minutes, applauding in the-- in the synagogue. This was something that-- that I'm very grateful for that I had this experience and reminded me of the kind of experience that-- that you can have, religious experiences here and now, even when you live well, in America. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were-- we were on the way to Ebensee. We were on the way from the [INAUDIBLE] factory to Ebensee. Now, after the nine months in-- in Wolfsberg, which compared-- looking back, this was like Gan Eden and-- and Gihennum, like the hell and-- and heaven, heaven and hell, such a difference. You come to Ebensee. Ebensee is a huge camp, meant for 20,000 people. But there is a gathering of 40,000 people because from all the neighboring camp that were running away from the Russian-- from the Russian occupation, they came to Ebensee. And Ebensee, they kept us in a quarantine block. This was block number 26. They kept us nude, nude, without clothes, without food. One of the Greeks broke a window and stretched out his hand to get a little-- a little-- a little snow from the window sill, and they shot his arm. One of the Salonikans. We called them the Greeks. Salonikans. They actually belonged to Greece. It still belongs to Greece. Salonika. We were there for three days without food, without anything. They would give us one-- one coffee, they would give us like lunchtime, and that's all. For three days. It was wintertime. We were huddled together in-- in-- in groups to keep our bodies warm. And the third day, they needed 50 men out of the 200 that we were there in that block. They needed 50 men to clean the snow around the camp so that the SS people can go to their watchtowers. And they gave us a jacket, a pair of pants, a pair of shoes, not even a pair of socks. It was winter, hard winter. This was Austria, where the winters are-- can be very hard. I remember that they gave me a shovel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think we had-- we had a cap on, a [INAUDIBLE] they gave us, a cap we had on. That was all the clothing that we have. And we worked for the whole day. I remember, when I kept the shovel in my hand, and I pushed the snow, and when I took off the hand to move it a little up or down, the skin would come off. It froze to the-- to the wood. It was so terribly cold. But believe it or not, I didn't even catch a cold. Unbelievable. Unbelievable how these things happen. I don't know. And the work there was so terribly hard. There were two baustelles, two places of work. One was like a death walk. They would throw-- you would walk between two-- two walls of-- of wire-- of wire [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fences. Two wire-- wire fences. I don't know if they were electrified or not. Didn't have to. On both sides were SS people, and all the way, they would throw big stones at you, from on side, from the other side, just for the fun of it. Just to kill you, just to hurt you. The going and the coming alone would be dangerous for life. I only worked there for two days. After two days, this brother-- camp brother of mine, he pulled me out from the line. I was always afraid. Where they put me, that's where I went. He pulled me out of the line by force and dragged me into the line where he was, into another line of-- group of people, where they went to the smaller-- smaller baustelle, it was a smaller working place which was close to the camp, about 10 minutes' walk from the camp. So that was already easier. I didn't have to walk that far. Besides that, there we were working inside the tunnels, and it was damp, and the work was very hard. And the Ukranians would beat you and take away your little bread. If you-- or if you had your [NON-ENGLISH], if they would like it, they would take that away. Whatever they could. They were stronger than us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember a case in Ebensee [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the-- in the nearby baustelle, where we worked. This was already very close to the liberating day. This must have been somewhere first of May, second of-- no, maybe-- maybe end of April, real end of April.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, we were working in a little place, and we see a spot of grass the size of-- of-- of-- smaller-- smaller than this room. The size-- a few feet. And all of us, we were like 30 people working there, all of us dropped our shovels and laid on our-- on our flats, on our stomachs, flat on our stomachs, and started with our-- to eat the grass with our mouth. And two SS men over the fence-- just a few feet away was the fence. Two SS men spoke Hungarian, and one says to the other, Peter, [NON-ENGLISH], Peter, come here. Come see this. They couldn't believe that we are so undernourished and so hungry that we, like the cattle, we laid down, and we didn't look if they are going to beat us or to do whatever. In a few minutes, you didn't see a piece of grass in that spot. Nothing. There was nothing left. We ate it up. Everything. The next day, I remember I had cramps in my stomach, and I fainted at work, and they said to bring me into the hospital. To bring me into the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: So two guys take me-- bring me to the hospital, and on the way, we meet two people-- two SS people. And they look at me, and they say, there is [SPEAKS GERMAN] he's finished. This one is also finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I say to myself, I will over live you. I am not kaput. I just fainted, but I feel strong. And when we came to near the barracks, I say to these two people that  I want to go-- I want to go to the bathroom. I went to the bathroom, and I ran away. I ran from one barrack to the other. I hid so they don't-- Because in the hospital, they would kill you definitely. That was the worst place to go. And I felt I'm weak enough, if they're-- they are not going to feed me, they are going to beat me up or something, then-- then I'm dead. So I ran away. I didn't want to go to the hospital. I didn't go to the hospital, and-- and that saved my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the next day, what happens? Somebody ran out from the-- from the appellplatz, where they were getting ready to work. They were missing somebody. Somebody ran out and came to the block. So the capo comes in, catches me. I said, I was the one who ran away, and gives me 25. Puts me over a stool.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, if you want to know what 25 means for a normal person at the beginning of the camp, when we were still relatively strong, they killed people by beating them 25. Now, I weighed already like 75 pounds. They are going to beat me 25. Everybody thought this is the end of Izchak Goldblatt. That's it. But I counted only to six, seven. I don't even know. They say, the-- my friends, those who were there, they say that I got 25. I counted six, seven. I wasn't even there. I didn't feel any pain. I don't know anything. I was saying my-- my psalms. My psalms. I wasn't even there. My body was there. And-- and then I got up after the 25, there was a non-Jewish blockalteste. He wanted to see my-- my tush. So he-- he made me take off my pants because how can you not scream when they beat you so hard. I said I didn't feel anything. He looked. He didn't see any sign on my tush. Unbelievable. I can't-- I can't imagine how this was. I know that at work, I wouldn't feel what they put on my shoulder. I was always praying, always sang. I knew a lot of-- most of the prayers I still know by heart. I knew all [SPEAKS HEBREW] by heart. At that time now, I don't know it, because I say less, but I knew all the psalms by heart. And all the time, I was praying and saying and saying and saying or mishna or Talmud. Whatever I knew by heart, I was always repeating that. And what they put on my shoulders or if they beat me, or if they hit me, I wasn't even aware of.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was my way of running away from-- from-- because people ask me how could you escape? And I said, I wasn't even there. Only my body was there. In spirit, if I was there, I couldn't have survived it because I was such a weak boy, such a slender little boy that at the beginning, they would take other boys would take-- I had to-- everybody had to carry 25 kilos of cement, to carry, and I couldn't carry it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would-- they would put the sack, I would fall underneath it. So the other boy would take one here and one here, and they would carry it for me. They would-- and all of those strong boys died because they were hungry. But I had, after I ate a little, I said, oh. I'm so fed. I'm so-- I'm-- I'm feeling so full. And I felt full. And they were thinking all the time, and they were talking all the time about food, and that killed them. I never talked about food. I was always praying. I talked to God. I had who to hold onto, and that's what saved me. And that's the only way I can explain that I got 25 without a-- without a--

speaker: interviewee
text: It reminds me of a little episode from Wolfsberg. We were working in Wolfsberg the first day, and I come out to work, and we were-- they were putting these cement sacks. 25 kilos-- that's like what? 50, 55 pounds, 60 pounds-- on my shoulder. And I would fall underneath the bag-- the bag. So and-- and then came that-- that non-German, the meister, the foreman, came and put this hand-- hand to my heart, and he found that it's beating like hell. It's beating like this. So he said, go there behind the-- there were some planks. Stay there and rest a little and have a-- and he tells somebody, give him a glass of tea. Sure enough, the largerkommandant walks by and sees somebody stays there is not working. So he comes over, puts his hand like Napoleon, here, and he says to me, what is it, junge? What is it? What do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, my heart. My heart. And-- and I put my hand, and I-- and he says, yeah, your heart. And suddenly, he gives me a [INAUDIBLE] on both-- with both hands. And the blood starts running from my nose. [SPEAKS GERMAN]! And I took that sack, and I started running with it like nobody else could run. So to show you what fear can do to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember another episode now that I talk about that fellow. That Kommandant was a [SPEAKS GERMAN]. Like a sergeant-major. We called him the shoemaker. Why the shoemaker? Because he would-- he would-- he would look at-- you had wooden shoes. Now, these wooden shoes, you walk one day to work and back, you-- you have to tread half of the-- half of the-- how do you call this part? Half of the sole, half of the heel you-- is-- is gone. And-- and if he catches you, when you came in, he would look at this, at your heels, and if it wasn't straight, he would pick you out and beat you, literally, to death. Literally to death.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I must tell a preliminary story before that. The-- every blockalteste had a [INAUDIBLE]. A [SPEAKS GERMAN] is what it's called, a servant, a personal servant. Now our lageralteste was a German guy by the name of Oskar, and he lived in the first tent of block number one with two-- two secretaries, also German Jewish people. This Oskar, one day I hear they called me. One Sunday, they called me, Izchak Goldblatt, Izchak Goldblatt. I'm very scared. If they call you by name, that means trouble only. So what happened, his [NON-ENGLISH], his-- his personal servant is a boy from Sighet, and he knew that I speak English, and they needed some translation for the Salonikan guys. They needed the chimney and the-- there-- the-- the tent was filling up with smoke. They couldn't speak one word of German, so I was translating from English to German, and for that, he gave me a little milk, about half a glass of milk on the spot, which was a half a glass of life. And every Sunday, ever second Sunday, when we were not working, every second Sunday we were off, he would leave his-- his plates, his and the two secretaries' plates for me to wash. So there would be left a little potato, a little fat. A little-- And I would wash them without detergents, with-- with-- with-- with soil, with sand. There was sand and water. I would work very hard to clean them, because the fat wouldn't come off if you didn't have some-- some detergent.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this lageralteste sympathized me because I spoke English, so he spoke to me as a [NON-ENGLISH] coming from a good house, and what have you studied? And he had a little schmooze with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, I come, and I go back to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the shoemaker, to this lagerkommandant, to the SS man. I come back, and he calls me out from the line. And I stay in line. And before-- before the block was-- was-- there was a ditch, kind of, going down. You had to run down that ditch. And the lageralteste saw me in-- in that line, where he kept the people who he's going to beat up because of the shoes. So he came behind me, and gave me with his foot, gave me a-- a-- a shtoop. Gave me a push. And he said, [SPEAKS GERMAN]. Run, because he's going to kill you. And Fischer, give him a push. [SPEAKS GERMAN] Fischer was the blockalteste who was responsible for the clothing, for the-- for the store, for the shoes that he kept-- that he kept the-- the staff. He was staff sergeant, kind of. And this Fischer throw me a pair of shoes, and I throw him one shoe, and I throw the other shoe, and I run out. And this guy is running after me, but he is old, like 50, 50 years probably, or 40 years, and I was a 17-year-old, very fast boy. And I ran around from one block to the other and came back. He never recognized me because we all looked alike. So these men saved my life. These lageralteste saved my life. And another episode of-- of what happened in the camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There we go.

speaker: interviewer
text: You seem like a natural--
